question.content: A thin glass tube with uniform cross-section and closed ends is bent into a vertical semicircle of radius r. A frictionless piston of mass m fits tightly in the tube and its angular position relative to the vertical is θ. On both sides of the piston there are n moles of ideal gas, and the gas temperature is always equal to the ambient absolute temperature T. Take gravity as g and the gas constant as R, and treat all gas-state changes as quasi-static isothermal processes. When T > Tc, θ=0 is a stable equilibrium; when T < Tc, the piston starts near θ=0 with negligible initial speed and moves away. Find the angular-speed magnitude at a reachable angle θ. Answer format: one final result only. If the result naturally simplifies to a single value, give that value; otherwise give a single algebraic expression using the symbols defined in the problem. Use ASCII characters only. Do not include units, explanatory text, or an equals sign. Answer format: one final result only. If the result naturally simplifies to a single value, give that value; otherwise give a single algebraic expression using the symbols defined in the problem. Do not include units, explanatory text, or an equals sign.

step_1.content: Define the coordinate θ on the semicircle and split both gas columns around the piston.
step_1.key_point: Track left and right arc lengths from the same center O.

step_3.content: Compare pressure effects on both sides and project all forces to the tangential direction.
step_3.key_point: Gas effect and gravity jointly control motion away from θ=0.

step_5.content: Accumulate tangential work from 0 to θ to obtain kinetic energy at the target state.
step_5.key_point: Use energy mapping between the start state and reachable state.

step_7.content: Convert kinetic energy to angular speed and keep the positive branch.
step_7.key_point: Report only the speed magnitude at angle θ.
